title: Afghanistan-Pakistan War Update — Apr 06, 2026
summary: Taliban and TTP fighters launched a coordinated ground assault on a Pakistani border post in Ghulam Khan Sector (North Waziristan) that was repelled, killing 37 attackers and wounding 80+. A separate TTP border infiltration attempt was foiled the same night with 8 militants killed. Pakistan announced cumulative Operation Ghazab-il-Haq toll of 796 Taliban killed and 1,043 wounded, figures hotly disputed by Kabul. Pakistan formally rejected UN human rights experts' claim of 'no credible evidence' linking TTP attacks to Afghan soil, citing multiple UN Security Council monitoring reports and SIGAR data. China-mediated peace talks in Urumqi opened April 2 with mid-level delegations from both sides — but Pakistani artillery and drone strikes on Kunar, Paktika, and Khost continued simultaneously, killing 2 children and wounding ~30 civilians and firing over 500 artillery shells into Kunar alone between April 2-4.

title: Afghanistan-Pakistan War Update — Apr 01, 2026
summary: Pakistan formally announced the full resumption of Operation Ghazab-il-Haq on March 31, declaring the campaign would continue 'until its objectives are achieved.' On March 30, the Pakistani Air Force conducted its widest single-day strike package of the conflict, hitting 41 locations including Taliban brigade and battalion headquarters in Nangarhar and an army HQ in Kandahar, killing 1 and wounding 16 civilians according to Afghan officials. UN Human Rights Chief Türk issued a formal condemnation calling the conflict a situation that 'piles misery on misery' and pleading for dialogue. The Torkham border crossing partially reopened on March 31 for Afghan national returns only, while trade and passenger services remained suspended. By April 1, the conflict had entered its fifth week with no ceasefire framework in sight, drawing CFR analysis describing it as a 'low-intensity interstate war' unlikely to resolve through negotiation in the near term.

title: Afghanistan-Pakistan War Update — Mar 29, 2026
summary: Pakistan formally resumed Operation Ghazab-il-Haq on March 26 following the Eid ceasefire collapse, with artillery strikes continuing in Kunar, Khost, Paktika and Nangarhar provinces. Human Rights Watch declared Pakistan's March 16 airstrike on the Omid Drug Rehabilitation Center in Kabul unlawful and a possible war crime, as the revised death toll reached 411 killed. A mass funeral was held in Kabul on March 26 with Interior Minister Haqqani vowing revenge. The Torkham border crossing briefly reopened for deportations on March 26 before being closed again after an Afghan sniper wounded a Pakistani Frontier Corps officer on the night of March 27. UNHCR confirmed 115,000 Afghans have been displaced since February 26, with no new ceasefire framework in sight.

title: Afghanistan-Pakistan War Update — Mar 26, 2026
summary: The five-day Eid al-Fitr ceasefire between Pakistan and Afghanistan, brokered by Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Turkey, collapsed on March 24 as cross-border artillery exchanges resumed within minutes of its expiry in Kunar and Zabul provinces, killing at least two Afghan civilians. Pakistan's Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar signaled no change in strategic posture, reaffirming commitment to 'eradicate terrorism' and leaving the door open to future military operations. The TTP simultaneously ended its own three-day Eid ceasefire, resuming attacks inside Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The UN has documented at least 289 civilian deaths in Afghanistan and 115,000 displaced since fighting intensified on February 26, following Pakistan's retaliatory airstrikes — including the March 16 strike on the Omid drug rehabilitation center in Kabul that killed at least 143 people. No new mediation framework is currently active.
